coredata.dc:title: Peptide-functionalized carbon dots for sensitive and selective Ca2+ detection
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Yuchen
Zheng, Yongfang
Guo, Yichuan
Yang, Yanlian
Li, Hongbian
Fang, Ying
Wang, Chen
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive and selective detection of calcium ions (Ca2+) is important since many biological processes are triggered and regulated by the change of Ca2+ concentrations. Here, we develop a new Ca2+ indicator based on peptide-functionalized carbon dots (f-CDs). The synthetic peptide was designed with a hydrophilic head that mimic the Ca2+ binding loop in calmodulin (CaM) protein, and a hydrophobic tail which can form stable interaction with CDs through π-π stacking. CDs functionalized with the synthetic peptide can specifically capture Ca2+ ions in biologically relevant media, leading to efficient fluorescence quenching. The quenched fluorescence intensity is linear with the concentration of Ca2+ ions over a wide range, from 5 × 10−6 to 10−2 M. Furthermore, cell viability assays show that f-CDs exhibit negligible cytotoxicity to neuroblastoma cells up to a concentration of 50 μg/mL. These results show that f-CDs are promising for sensitive and selective detection of Ca2+ ions in biological applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ca2+detection
Carbon dots
Peptide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of the free Ca2+ in serum
Photoluminescence stability of CDs under different conditions
Fabrication and characterization of peptide functionalized CDs (f-CDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: H. Li thanks the support from National Key R&D Program of China ( 2017YFF0209900 , 2017YFF0209901 ). Y. Fang thanks the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21790393 , 31600868 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterizations of CDs. (a) TEM image of CDs. The inset image is the size distribution histogram of the CDs. Scale bar, 20 nm. (b) HRTEM image of CDs. Scale bar, 2 nm. (c) FT-IR spectra of CDs and citric acid.

Optical properties of CDs. (a) Excitation and emission spectra of the CDs. The PL spectrum of the CDs was collected under 350 nm excitation. Inset shows a CD solution before (left) and after UV irradiation (right). (b–d) Fluorescent intensity of CDs at 470 nm under different pH values, ionic strength, and irradiation time. CDs were excited at 350 nm. The concentration of CDs is 100 μg/mL.

Functionalization of CDs with F5CB peptide. (a) Chemical structure of F5CB peptide. Asterisks highlight the six oxygen atoms coordinating a Ca2+ ion. (b) Schematic illustration of peptide functionalization of CDs. (c) Circular dichroism spectra of F5CB peptide and f-CDs.

Sensitive and selective Ca2+ detection with f-CDs. (a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and f-CDs in the presence of 5 μM Ca2+ ions. Both CDs and f-CDs were excited at 350 nm. (b) Fluorescent spectra of f-CDs at various Ca2+ concentrations. (c) Linear relationship between ΔF/F0 and Ca2+ concentration. (d) Ca2+ selectivity of f-CDs.
The detection of Ca2+ in serum. (a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the f-CDs in serum with and without free Ca2+; (b) Comparison of the free Ca2+ concentration determined by f-CDs and ICP-OES.

Biocompatibility of f-CDs. (a) Fluorescence microscopy imaging of nuclei DNA staining (DAPI) of SH-SY5Y cells treated with f-CDs for 72 h. Scale bar, 100 μm; (b) Viability of SH-SY5Y cells incubated with f-CDs at different concentrations for 24 h and 72 h.

head.ce:title: Peptide-functionalized carbon dots for sensitive and selective Ca2+ detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory for Biomedical Effects of Nanomaterials and Nanosafety
CAS Center for Excellence in Nanoscience
National Center for Nanoscience and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive and selective detection of calcium ions (Ca2+) is important since many biological processes are triggered and regulated by the change of Ca2+ concentrations. Here, we develop a new Ca2+ indicator based on peptide-functionalized carbon dots (f-CDs). The synthetic peptide was designed with a hydrophilic head that mimic the Ca2+ binding loop in calmodulin (CaM) protein, and a hydrophobic tail which can form stable interaction with CDs through π-π stacking. CDs functionalized with the synthetic peptide can specifically capture Ca2+ ions in biologically relevant media, leading to efficient fluorescence quenching. The quenched fluorescence intensity is linear with the concentration of Ca2+ ions over a wide range, from 5 × 10−6 to 10−2 M. Furthermore, cell viability assays show that f-CDs exhibit negligible cytotoxicity to neuroblastoma cells up to a concentration of 50 μg/mL. These results show that f-CDs are promising for sensitive and selective detection of Ca2+ ions in biological applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Calcium (Ca2+) is the most common signal transduction messenger in cells [1,2]. The concentration of free intracellular Ca2+ in resting cells is usually below 10−6 M, whereas that in extracellular fluid is about 10−3 M [3]. The intracellular Ca2+ concentration can increase by 1–2 orders of magnitude after cell activation. Ca2+ signaling regulates a multitude of physiological processes, including fertilization, secretion, smooth muscle contraction, sensory perception, and neuronal signaling [1,4]. Abnormalities in Ca2+ signaling can lead to severe pathological diseases, such as neurodegeneration and heart disease [5,6]. Therefore, sensitive and selective Ca2+ detection over a wide concentration range is highly desirable for biological applications.

Over past decades, various Ca2+ indicators have been developed, including fluorescent indicator dyes [7], fluorescent indicator proteins [8], and indicator quantum dots (QDs) [9,10]. These indicators exhibit altered fluorescent emission when bound with Ca2+. As a result, the concentrations of Ca2+ can be quantitatively determined from changes in the fluorescent emission of the indicators. Fluorescent indicator dyes have been chemically engineered with a broad range of Ca2+ affinities and various excitation wavelengths [7,11]. As a result, they have been widely applied for both in vitro and in vivo investigation of Ca2+ signaling. However, the fluorescent signals of indicator dyes decrease rapidly with time due to the photobleaching and leakage of dyes, limiting their use in long-term studies [12]. Genetically encoded indicator proteins are formed spontaneously within cells and much less susceptible to leakage [13,14]. However, indicator proteins have limited brightness and sensitivity to Ca2+ ions [15]. Recently, QD-based Ca2+ indicators have been developed [9,10]. QD indicators can selectively bind Ca2+ ions through the functional groups on their surface, including conjugated EGTA and Schiff base receptor [9,10]. The binding of Ca2+ quenches the fluorescence of the QDs. Compared to dye and protein indicators, QD indicators have the advantages of excellent photostability, high fluorescence brightness, and broad excitation spectra. However, most QDs contain toxic heavy-metal elements, such as cadmium, which greatly limits their biological applications.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted great attention due to their unique structure and interesting properties [16,17]. Particularly, there are many oxygen-containing functional groups, such as epoxy, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups, on the surface of CDs. As a result, radiative recombination of excitons located at the surface energy traps leads to strong fluorescence emission [18,19]. Therefore, CDs are emerging as promising alternatives to semiconductor QDs. In addition, the oxygen-containing groups on CDs can interact with metal ions to form coordination complexes, which quenches the fluorescence emission of CDs by electron transfer. As a result, CDs have been used as fluorescent indicators for the detection of a variety of ions from heavy metal ions, such as Hg2+, Cu2+ and Cd2+, to transition metal ions, such as Fe3+ and Zn2+ [17,20–24]. However, Ca2+ has a rather low binding affinity to the oxygen-containing groups on CDs, and it is rather difficult to detect Ca2+ in cells with high sensitivity [25]. Therefore, CD-based Ca2+ sensors with high sensitivity and selectivity are urgently required to be developed.

Calmodulin (CaM) is a ubiquitous Ca2+ sensor in eukaryotic cells [14]. The binding of Ca2+ in CaM is achieved by the EF-hand Ca2+ binding motif, which has a helix-loop-helix structure. Inspired by the Ca2+ sensing process of the CaM, we aim to develop a new CD-based Ca2+ sensor by using CaM mimic peptide as the functional groups. The peptide chelates the Ca2+ with high affinity in the solution. The capture of Ca2+ on the CDs leads to an electron transfer process, which results in the fluorescence quenching of the CDs. The as-prepared f-CDs exhibit high sensitivity, high selectivity and a wide linearity from 5 × 10−6 to 10−2 M towards Ca2+. In addition, the f-CD sensor is highly biocompatible, and negligible cytotoxicity was found to neuroblastoma cells up to a concentration of 50 μg/mL.

CDs were prepared according to a reported thermal pyrolysis method [26]. Briefly, 2 g citric acid (9.5 mmol) was heated at its melting point in a round-bottom flask in a heating mantle for 25 min, until a dark orange solution was obtained. The solution was neutralized with NaOH (10 mg/mL) to a pH of 7.0. A dialysis process (1000 Da) was performed with ultrapure water for 3 days to remove inorganic ions. The dialyzed solution was then freeze-dried to obtain CD powder with a dark-brown color.

1 mg F5CB peptide (NH2-FFFFFGGDKDGDGTIGADE-CONH2) with a purity>98% (Bankpeptide, Hefei, China) was firstly dissolved by adding 0.1 mol/L NaOH drop by drop and then diluted to 1 mL with ultrapure water. Then CD and peptide solutions were mixed and incubated at room temperature under gentle shaking for 1 h. The product was dried and kept at room temperature.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a FEI Tecnai F20 operating at 200 kV. Fluorescence spectra were collected on a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra and inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) were collected on a Perkin Elmer spectrum. Photostability experiments were performed on a molecular devices spectra max i3x multi-mode detection platform. Circular dichroism spectra of both the F5CB peptide and f-CDs (mass ratio of F5CB:CD = 1:20) were collected on a JASCO J-1500 circular dichroism spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of the free Ca2+ in serum
ce:section.ce:para:
Human serum sample was provided by Peking Union Medical College. The raw sample was centrifuged at 10, 000 rpm for 10 min to remove blood cell debris and other insoluble components. The supernatant was collected for analysis. 10 μL 10 mg/mL f-CDs was diluted in TBS buffer to a total volume of 60 μL and then added to 1 mL serum. The fluorescence spectrum was collected as previously described. For the fluorescence measurement of f-CDs in serum without free Ca2+, 50 μL 200 mM EGTA tetrasodium in TBS buffer was added to 1 mL serum before the addition of f-CDs. The concentration of the free Ca2+ in serum was determined as
c(Ca2+)=c (Ca2+)measured ×1.06
The concentration of free Ca2+ in serum was also determined with ICP-OES. The serum was ultrafiltrated for 20 min (MWCO 3000), and the permeate without bound Ca2+ was collected for analysis.

f-CDs were diluted in cell culture medium at desired concentrations. Neuroblastoma cells (SH-SY5Y) were maintained in RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 15% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (Life Technologies). Cells were digested, resuspended, and seeded on 96-well plates with a density about 5000 per well. After 24 h, the cells were administrated with f-CDs for another 24 h or 72 h. The cell viability of the f-CDs was evaluated by MTS assay, following the instructions of CellTiter 96® AQueous Non-Radioactive Cell Proliferation Assay (Promega, USA). The cells were then fixed and stained with DNA-specific 4',6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI). Fluorescence imaging was conducted using an Olympus IX 73 fluorescence microscopy.

Fig. 1 a shows a representative TEM image of the monodispersed CDs. The CDs are spherical with an average size of ∼3 nm. Fig. 1b is the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the CDs. The measured in-plane lattice spacing of 0.24 nm matches the (100) plane of graphite [20], which confirms the successful carbonization of the citric acid. In addition, FT-IR spectroscopy was used to further characterize the structural information of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 1c, the CDs show characteristic absorption bands of COC at 1200 cm−1, CO at 1717 cm−1, and −COOH at 1409 cm−1, indicating the presence of oxygen-containing groups on the CDs [23]. As a result, the CDs have good hydrophilicity and can be readily dispersed in aqueous solutions. No aggregates were observed in the CD solutions after 6 months.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence stability of CDs under different conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we investigated the optical properties of the CDs in aqueous media. Fig. 2 a is the excitation and emission spectra of the CDs. The CDs have optimal excitation and emission wavelength of 350 nm and 470 nm. As a result, the CD solution shows bright blue emission under UV lamp. The strong fluorescence of the CDs may be ascribed to their abundant surface groups. The fluorescence stability of the CDs was further characterized under different conditions. As shown in Fig. 2b, there was no observable change in the fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs over a range of pH values between 5 to 10. In addition, the CDs showed no change in fluorescence intensity when the ionic strength was increased from 0.1 to 10×Tris-buffered saline (TBS, 10 mM Tris, 150 mM NaCl, pH = 7.4), indicating that Na+ ions have little effect on the fluorescence properties of the CDs (Fig. 2c). We also characterized the fluorescence emission of the CDs under different irradiation times. Importantly, no bleaching was observed for the CDs after 2 h irradiation (Fig. 2d), indicating the good fluorescence stability of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of peptide functionalized CDs (f-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: We next characterized the fluorescence responses of bare CDs toward Ca2+ ions. CDs were dispersed in TBS buffer at a concentration of 100 μg/mL. The fluorescence of the CDs showed no observable change upon increasing the Ca2+ concentration from 0 to 10−2 M (Fig. S1). The low sensitivity of bare CDs toward Ca2+ ions can be ascribed to their low binding affinity. To increase the binding affinity of the CDs to Ca2+ ions, we functionalized the surface of the CDs with a synthetic peptide, F5CB. The F5CB peptide was designed to mimic the Ca2+-binding loop of CaM protein. The sequence of the F5CB peptide was NH2-FFFFFGGDKDGDGTIGADE-CONH2. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the F5CB peptide consists of a hydrophobic tail and a hydrophilic head. The hydrophobic tail (FFFFF) of the F5CB peptide can attach to the CDs through stable π-π stacking (Fig. 3b) [27]. The 12-amino acid head region of the F5CB peptide, DKDGDGTIGADE, corresponds of the Ca2+-binding loop of CaM. The F5CB peptide can coordinate with a Ca2+ ion through six oxygen atoms indicated in Fig. 3a [28].

We characterized the effect of peptide functionalization on the fluorescence properties of the CDs. As shown in Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs decreases with the increased F5CB-to-CD mass ratio, possibly due to intermolecular charge transfer between F5CB and CDs. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs reached a relatively constant value at a F5CB-to-CD mass ratio of 1:20, indicating the saturated adsorption of the F5CB peptide on CDs. Former studies have shown that peptides are prone to undergo conformation changes when interacting with carbon nanomaterials [27,29]. Thus, we further characterized the FT-IR spectra of both the F5CB peptide and f-CDs. The amide I band of the pristine F5CB peptide shifted from 1628 cm−1 to 1694 cm−1 after adsorption onto CDs (Fig. S3), suggesting that the F5CB peptide transferred from a random coil structure to a more ordered structure [27]. Circular dichroism spectroscopy further confirmed the conformation changes of the F5CB peptide. As shown in Fig. 3c, the pristine F5CB peptide shows a negative peak around 195 nm, indicating that the F5CB peptide is mainly random coil. On the other hand, a negative peak appears around 200 nm in the circular dichroism spectrum of the f-CDs, which shows that the F5CB peptide has organized into a more ordered β-structure on f-CDs [30].

Next, we compared the fluorescence responses of the CDs and f-CDs (both are 100 μg/mL) towards Ca2+ ions. Fig. 4 a shows the fluorescence emission spectra of both CDs and f-CDs in response to 5 μM Ca2+ ions. There is negligible change in the fluorescence emission of the CDs in the presence of 5 μM Ca2+ ions. On the other hand, we observed a clear fluorescence quenching of the f-CD upon the addition of 5 μM Ca2+ ions. The peptide groups on the f-CDs bind Ca2+ ions with high affinity. Following the capture of Ca2+, an electron transfer process may occur between Ca2+ and CDs, which inhibits the radiative recombination of excitons and leads to the fluorescence quenching of the CDs. Fig. 4b reveals the quenched fluorescence emission of f-CDs at various concentrations of Ca2+ ions. The fluorescence intensity of the f-CDs decreased gradually when the Ca2+ concentration was increased from 5 × 10−6 to 10−2 M. Fig.4c summarizes the quenched fluorescence (ΔF/F0) of the f-CDs at 470 nm as a function of Ca2+ concentrations. A good linear correlation was obtained over the Ca2+ concentration range from 5 × 10−6 to 10−2 M. In addition, f-CDs demonstrated high selectivity for Ca2+ over K+, Na+ and Mg2+ ions (Fig. 4d). These results confirm that f-CDs are promising for sensitive and selective detection of Ca2+ ions in biological relevant media.

To investigate the practical application of the sensor, the f-CDs were employed for the quantitative detection of the free Ca2+ in serum. As shown in Fig. 5 a, a rapid fluorescence quenching of the f-CDs was observed in serum. The concentration of the free Ca2+ in the serum was determined to be 1.18 ± 0.20 mM, which is consistent with the 1.01 ± 0.01 mM obtained by ICP-OES (Fig. 5b).

The biocompatibility of f-CDs is crucial for their biological applications. We further characterized the cytotoxicity of f-CDs to cells. Neuroblastoma cells (SH-SY5Y) were cultured in the presence of f-CDs. Fig. 6 a shows the fluorescence images of the DAPI stained SH-SY5Y cells after being incubated with f-CDs of different concentrations for 72 h. There is no apparent fluorescence intensity change for the cells treated by f-CDs from 0 to 50 μg/mL. MTS assays result in Fig. 6b shows that the cell viability shows no observable change up to a f-CD concentration of 50 μg/mL, which confirms that f-CDs have low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility.

In summary, peptide functionalized-CDs have been developed for sensitive and selective Ca2+ detection in biological relevant media. The peptide was designed to mimic the Ca2+-chelation loop of CaM for specific recognition of Ca2+ ions. As a result, the f-CDs showed efficient fluorescence quenching upon the binding of Ca2+ ions. The f-CDs exhibited Ca2+-dependent fluorescence that was linear over a large dynamic range from 5 × 10−6 to 10−2 M. Moreover, the f-CDs are highly biocompatible, as confirmed by their negligible cytotoxicity on neuroblastoma cells at a concentration up to 50 μg/mL. These characteristics, combined with their ease of synthesis and high fluorescence stability, make f-CDs promising candidates for sensitive Ca2+ detection in biological samples.
The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: H. Li thanks the support from National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFF0209900, 2017YFF0209901). Y. Fang thanks the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21790393, 31600868).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.07.073.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Fig. S1: (a) Fluorescence emission spectra of as-prepared CDs in the presence of Ca2+ ions of various concentrations. (b) The ΔF/F0 of CDs as a dependence of the Ca2+ concentration from 5 μM to 10 mM; Fig. S2: Fluorescence emission spectra of f-CDs at different F5CB-to-CD mass ratios. Fig. S3: FT-IR spectra of F5CB and f-CDs.

A comparison of fluorescent Ca2+ indicator properties and their use in measuring elementary and global Ca2+ signals

Fluorometric detection of Ca2+ based on an induced change in the conformation of sol-gel entrapped parvalbumin

Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence

Surface chemistry routes to modulate the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots: from fluorescence mechanism to up-conversion bioimaging applications
Synthesis of a carbon-dot-based photoluminescent probe for selective and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in water and living cells
Chemically tailoring coal to fluorescent carbon dots with tuned size and their capacity for Cu(II) detection
Highly selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ with lysine enhancing bovine serum albumin modified-carbon dots fluorescent probe

Programmable molecular composites of tandem proteins with graphene oxide for efficient bimorph actuators
Peptide-induced affinity binding of carbonic anhydrase to carbon nanotubes

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yuchen Linobtained his B.S. from Peking University in 2012 and is currently working toward a Ph.D. in nanoscience and technology at National Center for Nanoscience and Technology. His research interests focus on peptide-based nanostructures and their applications for modulating and tracking cellular signals transduction.

Yongfang Zheng obtained her B.S. degree from Lanzhou Univesity in 2015 and is pursuing a Ph.D. degree in physical chemistry at National Center for Nanoscience and Technology. Her research interests mainly focus on peptide assembly.

Yichuan Guo received his B.S. degree from Shandong University in 2013 and MS degree in physical chemistry in National Center for Nanoscience and Technology in 2018. His current research interest is the fabrication of flexible neural electrodes for brain activity mapping.

Yanlian Yang received B.S. and M.S. degrees in Chemistry from Shandong University in 1996 and 1999, respectively. In 2002, she obtained a Ph.D. degree in physical chemistry from Peking University, and was then a postdoctoral researcher in Peking University from 2002 to 2004. She was a visiting scholar at the Center for Biomedical Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology from 2007 to 2008. She is currently a professor in National Center for Nanoscience and Technology. Her research interests include molecular and nanomaterial self-assembly, and application of nanotechnology for highly sensitive diagnosis of cancer and neurodegenerative diseases.

Hongbian Li received her Ph.D. degree in inorganic chemistry from Nanjing University in 2008. She got further trained as a Postdoc. researcher in Peking University from 2009 to 2011. She is currently an associate professor in National Center for Nanoscience and Technology of China. Her current research interest is carbon-based sensors for flexible electronics.

Ying Fang received her B.S. degree from University of Science and Technology of China. Then she got her Master and Ph.D. degrees from Harvard University. Currently, she is a professor in National Center for Nanoscience and Technology of China (NCNST). Her research interests mainly focus on nanomaterials and flexible bioelectronics.

Chen Wang received his B.S. degree from University of Science and Technology of China in 1986, and Ph.D. degree from University of Virginia in 1992. He joined Arizona State University as a postdoctoral associate. He joined the faculty of Central China Normal University in 1994, and Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences from 1995 to 2004. He is currently a professor in National Center for Nanoscience and Technology of China since 2004. His research interests include surface physical chemistry and nano-bio interface.
